items.fix: Include a column of ones in X or set fit_intercept=True / nn.Linear bias=True so the model can shift ŷ off zero.
items.why: Without b, the hyperplane is forced through the origin, skewing w and systematically biasing predictions when y has a nonzero baseline.
items.example: House prices never near $0 at 0 sq ft — LinearRegression() with default intercept vs forcing through origin gives wildly wrong baseline.

items.mistake: Using linear regression on classification labels
items.fix: Use logistic regression or another classifier with cross-entropy when y is discrete classes, not continuous numbers.
items.why: MSE on 0/1 labels treats them as a numeric scale; predictions like 0.73 are not valid class decisions without a threshold framework.
items.example: Wrong: LinearRegression on churn 0/1. Right: LogisticRegression — see Regression vs Classification.

items.mistake: Inverting XᵀX when features are collinear
items.fix: Use np.linalg.lstsq, sklearn LinearRegression, ridge regularization, or drop redundant columns before raw (XᵀX)⁻¹.
items.why: Perfect correlation (e.g. sq ft and sq m) makes XᵀX singular; naive inv() raises LinAlgError or returns unstable w.
items.example: X = [sq_ft, sq_ft * 2.0] → use lstsq or remove duplicate feature.

items.fix: Report RMSE/MAE on held-out validation data; check residual plots for nonlinearity and heteroscedasticity.
items.why: OLS always fits training labels as well as linearly possible — low train MSE does not prove generalization.
items.example: Train MSE = 1000, test RMSE = 50000 → overfitting to noise or distribution shift, not a deployment-ready model.

items.fix: Clip predictions, flag out-of-range inputs, or use models with sensible bounds; treat linear trends as local approximations.
items.why: Linear w continues trending forever — predicting price for 50× max sq ft in training can yield nonsense ŷ.
items.example: Max training sq ft = 3000; predict at 20000 → model may output negative or absurd millions without guardrails.
